GitHub

Getting Started with xAct.jl

This tutorial introduces the core concepts of xAct.jl and shows how to perform basic tensor algebra operations. We also demonstrate how the same operations look in Python using the sxact wrapper.

1. Setup

First, we load the xAct module (the Julia port of the xAct suite).

using xAct

We also load PythonCall to demonstrate the polyglot nature of the project.

using PythonCall
    CondaPkg Found dependencies: /var/home/sasha/.julia/packages/PythonCall/83z4q/CondaPkg.toml
    CondaPkg Found dependencies: /var/home/sasha/.julia/packages/CondaPkg/8GjrP/CondaPkg.toml
    CondaPkg Initialising pixi
             │ /var/home/sasha/.julia/artifacts/cefba4912c2b400756d043a2563ef77a0088866b/bin/pixi
             │ init
             │ --format pixi
             └ /var/home/sasha/para/areas/dev/gh/sk/sxAct/docs/.CondaPkg
✔ Created /var/home/sasha/para/areas/dev/gh/sk/sxAct/docs/.CondaPkg/pixi.toml
    CondaPkg Wrote /var/home/sasha/para/areas/dev/gh/sk/sxAct/docs/.CondaPkg/pixi.toml
             │ [dependencies]
             │ openssl = ">=3, <3.6"
             │ libstdcxx = ">=3.4,<=15.1"
             │ libstdcxx-ng = ">=3.4,<=15.1"
             │
             │     [dependencies.python]
             │     channel = "conda-forge"
             │     build = "*cp*"
             │     version = ">=3.10,!=3.14.0,!=3.14.1,<4"
             │
             │ [project]
             │ name = ".CondaPkg"
             │ platforms = ["linux-64"]
             │ channels = ["conda-forge"]
             │ channel-priority = "strict"
             └ description = "automatically generated by CondaPkg.jl"
    CondaPkg Installing packages
             │ /var/home/sasha/.julia/artifacts/cefba4912c2b400756d043a2563ef77a0088866b/bin/pixi
             │ install
             └ --manifest-path /var/home/sasha/para/areas/dev/gh/sk/sxAct/docs/.CondaPkg/pixi.toml
✔ The default environment has been installed.

2. Defining a Manifold

In the original Wolfram xAct, you would use: DefManifold[M, 4, {a, b, c, d, e, f}]

In sxAct.jl, we use def_manifold!. The ! indicates that this function modifies the global session state.

M = def_manifold!(:M, 4, [:a, :b, :c, :d, :e, :f])
ManifoldObj(:M, 4, [:a, :b, :c, :d, :e, :f])

Let's check the dimension:

println("Manifold M dimension: ", Dimension(:M))
Manifold M dimension: 4

3. Defining Tensors

Now we define a symmetric rank-2 tensor $T_{ab}$. In Wolfram: DefTensor[T[-a, -b], M, Symmetric[{-a, -b}]]

T = def_tensor!(:T, ["-a", "-b"], :M, symmetry_str="Symmetric[{-a,-b}]")
TensorObj(:T, IndexSpec[IndexSpec(:a, true), IndexSpec(:b, true)], :M, SymmetrySpec(:Symmetric, [1, 2], Int64[]))

4. Canonicalization

One of the most powerful features of xAct is its ability to canonicalize tensor expressions using the Butler-Portugal algorithm.

Consider the expression $T_{ba} - T_{ab}$. Since $T$ is symmetric, this should be zero.

expr = "T[-b, -a] - T[-a, -b]"
canonical = ToCanonical(expr)
println("Canonical form of '$expr': ", canonical)
Canonical form of 'T[-b, -a] - T[-a, -b]': 0

5. The Python Bridge

sxAct is designed to be accessible from Python. Using PythonCall.jl, we can see how a Python user would interact with the same core.

import sxact
M = sxact.Manifold("M", 4)
T = sxact.Tensor("T", ["-a", "-b"], M, symmetry="Symmetric")
print(sxact.to_canonical("T[-b,-a] - T[-a,-b]"))

Note: The high-level Python API shown above is a goal for the next development phase. Currently, the Python side is primarily used for verification and testing.

6. Summary Table

OperationWolfram (xAct)Julia (sxAct.jl)
DefManifoldDefManifold[M, 4, {a,b}]def_manifold!(:M, 4, [:a, :b])
DefTensorDefTensor[T[-a,-b], M]def_tensor!(:T, ["-a", "-b"], :M)
ToCanonicalToCanonical[expr]ToCanonical(expr)

This page was generated using Literate.jl.
